runs.raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of what changed and why:

**Key changes from the old `ClusterPolicy` to the new `ValidatingPolicy`:**

| Old (`kyverno.io/v1 ClusterPolicy`) | New (`policies.kyverno.io/v1 ValidatingPolicy`) |
|---|---|
| `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` | `spec.validationActions: [Audit]` |
| `spec.background: false` | `spec.evaluation.background.enabled: false` |
| `spec.rules[].match` | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` |
| `spec.rules[].preconditions` | `spec.matchConditions` (CEL expressions) |
| `spec.rules[].context[].apiCall` (JMESPath) | `spec.variables` using `resource.List()` CEL library |
| `spec.rules[].validate.deny.conditions` (JMESPath) | `spec.validations[].expression` (CEL) |

**Notable design decisions:**

- **`resource.List("policy/v1", "poddisruptionbudgets", object.metadata.namespace)`** replaces the old `apiCall` + JMESPath pattern to fetch PDBs in the same namespace as the incoming resource.
- **Label matching** is done in CEL by iterating over all `matchLabels` keys of the PDB selector and confirming they exist in the pod template labels with matching values — equivalent to the old `label_match()` JMESPath function.
- **`matchConditions`** replaces `preconditions`, using the optional chaining `?replicas.orValue(1)` CEL syntax to safely default a missing replicas field.
- **`messageExpression`** provides a dynamic, context-rich failure message (PDB name, actual values) in addition to the static `message` fallback.

runs.raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made:

**API/Kind changes:**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1`
- `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `kind: ValidatingPolicy`

**Spec restructuring:**
- `validationFailureAction: Audit` → `validationActions: [Audit]`
- `background: false` → `evaluation.background.enabled: false`
- `rules[].match` + `rules[].preconditions` → `matchConstraints.resourceRules` (CEL `matchConditions` handles the replica precondition)

**Context/external data:**
- The JMESPath `apiCall` context using `urlPath` + `jmesPath` is replaced with the Kyverno CEL `resource.List()` library function, which lists PodDisruptionBudgets in the resource's namespace
- PDB label matching is done in CEL using `.filter()` and `.all()` instead of the `label_match` JMESPath function

**Validation logic:**
- The `validate.deny.conditions` block is replaced with a CEL `validations[].expression` that passes when no matching PDB exists, the PDB has no `minAvailable`, or `replicas != minAvailable`
- A dynamic `messageExpression` is added to surface the PDB name, `minAvailable`, and replica count in the violation message

apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: pdb-minavailable-check
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Check PodDisruptionBudget minAvailable
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Other
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.16.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.27"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: PodDisruptionBudget, Deployment, StatefulSet
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      When a Pod controller which can run multiple replicas is subject to an active PodDisruptionBudget,
      if the replicas field has a value equal to the minAvailable value of the PodDisruptionBudget
      it may prevent voluntary disruptions including Node drains which may impact routine maintenance
      tasks and disrupt operations. This policy checks incoming Deployments and StatefulSets which have
      a matching PodDisruptionBudget to ensure these two values do not match.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: false
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [apps]
        apiVersions: [v1]
        operations: [CREATE, UPDATE]
        resources: [deployments, statefulsets]
  # Skip resources with 0 replicas (not subject to disruption concerns)
  matchConditions:
    - name: has-replicas
      expression: >-
        !has(object.spec.replicas) || object.spec.replicas > 0
  variables:
    - name: pdbs
      expression: >-
        resource.List("policy/v1", "poddisruptionbudgets", object.metadata.namespace).items
    - name: podLabels
      expression: >-
        object.spec.template.metadata.?labels.orValue({})
    # Find the first PDB whose selector.matchLabels is a subset of the pod template labels
    - name: matchingPdb
      expression: >-
        variables.pdbs.filter(pdb,
          has(pdb.spec.selector) &&
          has(pdb.spec.selector.matchLabels) &&
          pdb.spec.selector.matchLabels.all(k,
            k in variables.podLabels && pdb.spec.selector.matchLabels[k] == variables.podLabels[k]
          )
        )
    - name: minAvailable
      expression: >-
        variables.matchingPdb.size() > 0 && has(variables.matchingPdb[0].spec.minAvailable)
          ? variables.matchingPdb[0].spec.minAvailable
          : 0
    - name: replicas
      expression: >-
        has(object.spec.replicas) ? object.spec.replicas : 1
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.minAvailable == 0 || int(variables.minAvailable) != variables.replicas
      message: >-
        The matching PodDisruptionBudget for this resource has its minAvailable value equal to the
        replica count which is not permitted.
      messageExpression: >-
        "The matching PodDisruptionBudget has minAvailable=" + string(variables.minAvailable) +
        " which equals the replica count (" + string(variables.replicas) + "). " +
        "This may prevent voluntary disruptions including Node drains."

| Old (`kyverno.io/v1 ClusterPolicy`) | New (`policies.kyverno.io/v1 ValidatingPolicy`) |
|---|---|
| `validationFailureAction: Audit` | `validationActions: [Audit]` |
| `background: false` | `evaluation.background.enabled: false` |
| `match.any[].resources.kinds` | `matchConstraints.resourceRules` with `apiGroups: [apps]`, `apiVersions: [v1]`, `operations: [CREATE, UPDATE]` |
| `preconditions` (JMESPath + operators) | `matchConditions` (CEL expressions) |
| `context[].apiCall` with `jmesPath` | `variables` using `resource.List()` from Kyverno's CEL Resource library |
| `validate.deny.conditions` | `validations[].expression` (CEL boolean) |

**Logic translation highlights:**

- The JMESPath `label_match(spec.selector.matchLabels, ...)` is replaced with a pure CEL expression that checks each key in `pdb.spec.selector.matchLabels` is present and matches in the pod template labels.
- The `precondition` guarding `replicas > 0` becomes a `matchConditions` CEL expression (`!has(object.spec.replicas) || object.spec.replicas > 0`), which short-circuits policy evaluation entirely when not applicable.
- `minAvailable` defaults to `0` when no matching PDB is found (same semantics as the original `|| \`0\``).
- A `messageExpression` is added for a dynamic, contextual failure message showing the actual values that conflicted.

description: capabilities permit privileged actions without giving full root access
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_require_drop_all.yaml to a Kyverno 1.16+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy capabilities permit privileged actions without giving full root access. Write the converted policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/cp_require_drop_all.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.16+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: drop-all-capabilities
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Drop All Capabilities
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Capabilities permit privileged actions without giving full root access. All
      capabilities should be dropped from a Pod, with only those required added back.
      This policy ensures that all containers explicitly specify the `drop: ["ALL"]`
      ability. Note that this policy also illustrates how to cover drop entries in any
      case although this may not strictly conform to the Pod Security Standards.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
    - name: require-drop-all
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
              - Pod
      preconditions:
        all:
        - key: "{{ request.operation || 'BACKGROUND' }}"
          operator: NotEquals
          value: DELETE
      validate:
        message: >-
          Containers must drop `ALL` capabilities.
        foreach:
          - list: request.object.spec.[ephemeralContainers, initContainers, containers][]
            deny:
              conditions:
                all:
                - key: ALL
                  operator: AnyNotIn
                  value: "{{ element.securityContext.capabilities.drop[].to_upper(@) || `[]` }}"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: drop-all-capabilities
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Drop All Capabilities
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.16.0
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Capabilities permit privileged actions without giving full root access. All
      capabilities should be dropped from a Pod, with only those required added back.
      This policy ensures that all containers explicitly specify the `drop: ["ALL"]`
      ability. Note that this policy also illustrates how to cover drop entries in any
      case although this may not strictly conform to the Pod Security Standards.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
  variables:
    - name: allContainers
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers
        + object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([])
        + object.spec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.allContainers.all(container,
          container.?securityContext.?capabilities.?drop.orValue([]).exists(
            capability, capability.upperAscii() == 'ALL'
          )
        )
      message: "Containers must drop `ALL` capabilities."
raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made:

**API/Kind changes:**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1`
- `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `kind: ValidatingPolicy`

**Spec restructuring:**
- `validationFailureAction: Audit` → `validationActions: [Audit]` (the new list-based field)
- `background: true` is implicit; removed (handled by default `spec.evaluation` settings)
- The `rules[].match` block with `any`/`resources`/`kinds` is replaced by `matchConstraints.resourceRules`, following the `ValidatingAdmissionPolicy` pattern
- The `preconditions` guard against `DELETE` operations is replaced by only listing `CREATE` and `UPDATE` in `operations`

**Validation logic:**
- The `foreach`/`deny` construct (JMESPath-based) is converted to a CEL `expression` using the `all()` macro
- The `list: request.object.spec.[ephemeralContainers, initContainers, containers][]` iteration is replaced by a `variables.allContainers` CEL variable combining all three container lists with `?`-safe optional chaining and `orValue([])`
- The uppercase check (`to_upper(@)`) is replaced by CEL's `.upperAscii()` method on each `capability` string
- `variables` are now top-level under `spec` rather than nested inside rules

description: define and use labels that identify semantic attributes of your application or Deployment
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_require_labels.yaml to a Kyverno 1.16+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy define and use labels that identify semantic attributes of your application or Deployment. Write the converted policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/cp_require_labels.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.16+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-labels
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Labels
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod, Label
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Define and use labels that identify semantic attributes of your application or Deployment.
      A common set of labels allows tools to work collaboratively, describing objects in a common manner that
      all tools can understand. The recommended labels describe applications in a way that can be
      queried. This policy validates that the label `app.kubernetes.io/name` is specified with some value.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: check-for-labels
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "The label `app.kubernetes.io/name` is required."
      pattern:
        metadata:
          labels:
            app.kubernetes.io/name: "?*"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-labels
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Labels
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.16.0
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod, Label
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Define and use labels that identify semantic attributes of your application or Deployment.
      A common set of labels allows tools to work collaboratively, describing objects in a common manner that
      all tools can understand. The recommended labels describe applications in a way that can be
      queried. This policy validates that the label `app.kubernetes.io/name` is specified with some value.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: true
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        has(object.metadata.labels) && 'app.kubernetes.io/name' in object.metadata.labels && size(object.metadata.labels['app.kubernetes.io/name']) > 0
      messageExpression: >-
        "The label `app.kubernetes.io/name` is required on Pod " + object.metadata.name + "."
      message: "The label `app.kubernetes.io/name` is required."
raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made:

**API changes:**
- `apiVersion`: `kyverno.io/v1` → `policies.kyverno.io/v1`
- `kind`: `ClusterPolicy` → `ValidatingPolicy`

**Spec restructuring:**
- `validationFailureAction: Audit` → `validationActions: [Audit]`
- `background: true` → `evaluation.background.enabled: true`
- `spec.rules[].match` with `kinds: [Pod]` → `matchConstraints.resourceRules` using the standard Kubernetes admission webhook format (apiGroups, apiVersions, operations, resources)
- The pattern-based `validate.pattern` rule → a CEL `expression` under `validations`

**CEL expression:**
The original `pattern` check (`app.kubernetes.io/name: "?*"`) is expressed in CEL as:
```
has(object.metadata.labels) && 'app.kubernetes.io/name' in object.metadata.labels && size(object.metadata.labels['app.kubernetes.io/name']) > 0
```
This checks that the labels map exists, the key is present, and its value is non-empty — equivalent to the `?*` (one or more characters) pattern.

A `messageExpression` was also added to produce a dynamic, resource-specific failure message in PolicyReports.

- `spec.rules[].match` with `kinds: [Pod]` → `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` with explicit `apiGroups`, `apiVersions`, `operations`, and `resources`
- `spec.rules[].validate.pattern` (Kyverno pattern syntax) → `spec.validations[].expression` using CEL

**Validation logic**
The old `pattern` check (`app.kubernetes.io/name: "?*"`) is expressed in CEL as:
```
has(object.metadata.labels) && 'app.kubernetes.io/name' in object.metadata.labels && size(object.metadata.labels['app.kubernetes.io/name']) > 0
```
A `messageExpression` is also added to produce a dynamic, resource-specific failure message alongside the static fallback `message`.

yaml_preview:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-pdb
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require PodDisruptionBudget
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.16.0
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Deployment, StatefulSet, PodDisruptionBudget
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      PodDisruptionBudget resources are useful to ensuring minimum availability
      is maintained at all times. This policy checks all incoming Deployments and StatefulSets
      to ensure they have a matching, preexisting PodDisruptionBudget.
      Note: This policy must be run in enforce mode to ensure accuracy.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  evaluation:
    admission:
      enabled: true
    background:
      enabled: false
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ["apps"]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE"]
        resources: ["deployments", "statefulsets"]
  matchConditions:
    - name: has-min-replicas
      expression: "object.spec.?replicas.orValue(1) >= 3"
  variables:
    - name: podLabels
      expression: "object.spec.template.metadata.?labels.orValue({})"
    - name: pdbs
      expression: >-
        resource.List("policy/v1", "poddisruptionbudgets", object.metadata.namespace).items
    - name: matchingPdbCount
      expression: >-
        variables.pdbs.filter(pdb,
          has(pdb.spec.selector) &&
          has(pdb.spec.selector.matchLabels) &&
          pdb.spec.selector.matchLabels.all(k,
            k in variables.podLabels &&
            pdb.spec.selector.matchLabels[k] == variables.podLabels[k]
          )
        ).size()
  validations:
    - expression: "variables.matchingPdbCount >= 1"
      message: "There is no corresponding PodDisruptionBudget found for this Deployment."
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-pdb
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require PodDisruptionBudget
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.6.2
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.23"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Deployment, PodDisruptionBudget
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      PodDisruptionBudget resources are useful to ensuring minimum availability
      is maintained at all times. This policy checks all incoming Deployments and StatefulSets
      to ensure they have a matching, preexisting PodDisruptionBudget.
      Note: This policy must be run in `enforce` mode to ensure accuracy.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: false
  rules:
    - name: require-pdb
      match:
        any:
          - resources:
              kinds:
                - Deployment
                - StatefulSet
      preconditions:
        all:
          - key: "{{request.operation || 'BACKGROUND'}}"
            operator: Equals
            value: CREATE
          - key: "{{ request.object.spec.replicas }}"
            operator: GreaterThanOrEquals
            value: 3
      context:
        - name: pdb_count
          apiCall:
            urlPath: "/apis/policy/v1/namespaces/{{request.namespace}}/poddisruptionbudgets"
            jmesPath: "items[?label_match(spec.selector.matchLabels, `{{request.object.spec.template.metadata.labels}}`)] | length(@)"
      validate:
        message: "There is no corresponding PodDisruptionBudget found for this Deployment."
        deny:
          conditions:
            any:
              - key: "{{pdb_count}}"
                operator: LessThan
                value: 1

The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of what changed and why:

**Structural changes:**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` + `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` + `kind: ValidatingPolicy`
- `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` → `spec.validationActions: [Audit]`
- `spec.background: false` → `spec.evaluation.background.enabled: false` (explicit admission-only)
- `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources` → `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules[]` (VAP-style)

**Preconditions → matchConditions (CEL):**
- The operation=`CREATE` filter moved into `matchConstraints.resourceRules[].operations: [CREATE]`
- The `replicas >= 3` check became a CEL `matchCondition` using `object.spec.?replicas.orValue(1) >= 3` (safe optional chaining)

**Context API call → variables (CEL resource.List):**
- The JMESPath `apiCall` for listing PDBs became `resource.List("policy/v1", "poddisruptionbudgets", object.metadata.namespace).items` using the Kyverno CEL resource library
- The label matching logic (previously a JMESPath `label_match`) is expressed in CEL: iterating PDB `matchLabels` and checking each key exists in the pod's template labels with the same value

**Validate/deny → validations (CEL):**
- The `deny` condition (`pdb_count < 1`) is inverted to a positive `validations` expression: `variables.matchingPdbCount >= 1`

      enabled: false
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ["apps"]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE"]
        resources: ["deployments", "statefulsets"]
  matchConditions:
    - name: has-min-replicas
      expression: "object.spec.?replicas.orValue(1) >= 3"
  variables:
    - name: podLabels
      expression: "object.spec.template.metadata.?labels.orValue({})"
    - name: pdbs
      expression: >-
        resource.List("policy/v1", "poddisruptionbudgets", object.metadata.namespace).items
    - name: matchingPdbCount
      expression: >-
        variables.pdbs.filter(pdb,
          has(pdb.spec.selector) &&
          has(pdb.spec.selector.matchLabels) &&
          pdb.spec.selector.matchLabels.all(k,
            k in variables.podLabels &&
            pdb.spec.selector.matchLabels[k] == variables.podLabels[k]
          )
        ).size()
  validations:
    - expression: "variables.matchingPdbCount >= 1"
      message: "There is no corresponding PodDisruptionBudget found for this Deployment."

- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` + `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` + `kind: ValidatingPolicy`

description: liveness and readiness probes need to be configured to correctly manage a Pod's lifecycle during deployments, restarts, and upgrades
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_require_probes.yaml to a Kyverno 1.16+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy liveness and readiness probes need to be configured to correctly manage a Pod's lifecycle during deployments, restarts, and upgrades. Write the converted policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/cp_require_probes.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.16+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-pod-probes
  annotations:
    pod-policies.kyverno.io/autogen-controllers: DaemonSet,Deployment,StatefulSet
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Pod Probes
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Liveness and readiness probes need to be configured to correctly manage a Pod's
      lifecycle during deployments, restarts, and upgrades. For each Pod, a periodic
      `livenessProbe` is performed by the kubelet to determine if the Pod's containers
      are running or need to be restarted. A `readinessProbe` is used by Services
      and Deployments to determine if the Pod is ready to receive network traffic.
      This policy validates that all containers have one of livenessProbe, readinessProbe,
      or startupProbe defined.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-probes
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    preconditions:
      all:
      - key: "{{request.operation || 'BACKGROUND'}}"
        operator: AnyIn
        value:
        - CREATE
        - UPDATE
    validate:
      message: "Liveness, readiness, or startup probes are required for all containers."
      foreach:
      - list: request.object.spec.containers[]
        deny:
          conditions:
            all:
            - key: livenessProbe
              operator: AllNotIn
              value: "{{ element.keys(@)[] }}"
            - key: startupProbe
              operator: AllNotIn
              value: "{{ element.keys(@)[] }}"
            - key: readinessProbe
              operator: AllNotIn
              value: "{{ element.keys(@)[] }}"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-pod-probes
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Pod Probes
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Liveness and readiness probes need to be configured to correctly manage a Pod's
      lifecycle during deployments, restarts, and upgrades. For each Pod, a periodic
      `livenessProbe` is performed by the kubelet to determine if the Pod's containers
      are running or need to be restarted. A `readinessProbe` is used by Services
      and Deployments to determine if the Pod is ready to receive network traffic.
      This policy validates that all containers have one of livenessProbe, readinessProbe,
      or startupProbe defined.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ['']
        apiVersions: ['v1']
        operations: ['CREATE', 'UPDATE']
        resources: ['pods']
  autogen:
    podControllers:
      controllers:
        - DaemonSet
        - Deployment
        - StatefulSet
  variables:
    - name: allContainers
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers
        + object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([])
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.allContainers.all(c,
          has(c.livenessProbe) || has(c.readinessProbe) || has(c.startupProbe)
        )
      messageExpression: >-
        "Liveness, readiness, or startup probes are required for all containers. " +
        "Missing probe in container(s): " +
        variables.allContainers.filter(c,
          !has(c.livenessProbe) && !has(c.readinessProbe) && !has(c.startupProbe)
        ).map(c, c.name).join(", ")

The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made:

**API/Kind changes:**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1`
- `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `kind: ValidatingPolicy`

**Spec structure changes:**
- `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` → `spec.validationActions: [Audit]`
- Removed `spec.background: true` — replaced by the `autogen` + `matchConstraints` approach
- Removed `spec.rules[]` with its `match`/`preconditions`/`validate.foreach` structure; replaced with the Kubernetes-native `matchConstraints.resourceRules` (which implicitly covers CREATE/UPDATE operations)

**Logic changes (JMESPath → CEL):**
- The `foreach` + `deny` block using JMESPath (`element.keys(@)[]`, `AllNotIn`) is replaced by a single CEL `validations[].expression` using `variables.allContainers.all(c, has(c.livenessProbe) || ...)` — idiomatic CEL with `has()` for field existence checks
- A `variables` block pre-computes `allContainers` (containers + initContainers) following the pattern from the Kyverno CEL library docs
- `messageExpression` dynamically names which containers are missing probes, replacing the static `message` string

**Auto-generation:**
- Replaced the `pod-policies.kyverno.io/autogen-controllers` annotation with `spec.autogen.podControllers.controllers` (the structured 1.16+ approach), preserving DaemonSet/Deployment/StatefulSet coverage

description: as application workloads share cluster resources, it is important to limit resources requested and consumed by each Pod
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_require_resource_limits.yaml to a Kyverno 1.16+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy as application workloads share cluster resources, it is important to limit resources requested and consumed by each Pod. Write the converted policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/cp_require_resource_limits.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.16+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-requests-limits
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Limits and Requests
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      As application workloads share cluster resources, it is important to limit resources
      requested and consumed by each Pod. It is recommended to require resource requests and
      limits per Pod, especially for memory and CPU. If a Namespace level request or limit is specified,
      defaults will automatically be applied to each Pod based on the LimitRange configuration.
      This policy validates that all containers have something specified for memory and CPU
      requests and memory limits.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-resources
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "CPU and memory resource requests and memory limits are required for containers."
      pattern:
        spec:
          containers:
          - resources:
              requests:
                memory: "?*"
                cpu: "?*"
              limits:
                memory: "?*"
          =(initContainers):
          - resources:
              requests:
                memory: "?*"
                cpu: "?*"
              limits:
                memory: "?*"
          =(ephemeralContainers):
          - resources:
              requests:
                memory: "?*"
                cpu: "?*"
              limits:
                memory: "?*"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-requests-limits
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Limits and Requests
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.16.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      As application workloads share cluster resources, it is important to limit resources
      requested and consumed by each Pod. It is recommended to require resource requests and
      limits per Pod, especially for memory and CPU. If a Namespace level request or limit is specified,
      defaults will automatically be applied to each Pod based on the LimitRange configuration.
      This policy validates that all containers have something specified for memory and CPU
      requests and memory limits.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ['']
        apiVersions: [v1]
        operations: [CREATE, UPDATE]
        resources: [pods]
  variables:
    - name: allContainers
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers
        + object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([])
        + object.spec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.allContainers.all(container,
          has(container.resources) &&
          has(container.resources.requests) &&
          has(container.resources.requests.memory) &&
          has(container.resources.requests.cpu) &&
          has(container.resources.limits) &&
          has(container.resources.limits.memory)
        )
      message: "CPU and memory resource requests and memory limits are required for containers."
      messageExpression: >-
        "Container '" +
        variables.allContainers.filter(container,
          !(has(container.resources) &&
            has(container.resources.requests) &&
            has(container.resources.requests.memory) &&
            has(container.resources.requests.cpu) &&
            has(container.resources.limits) &&
            has(container.resources.limits.memory))
        )[0].name +
        "' is missing required CPU/memory requests or memory limits."
raw_log:
The converted policy is written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made during conversion:

**API / Kind**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` + `ClusterPolicy` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` + `ValidatingPolicy`

**Enforcement mode**
- `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` → `spec.validationActions: [Audit]`

**Match constraints**
- The old `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources` block is replaced by `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules`, which follows the Kubernetes `ValidatingAdmissionPolicy` structure and explicitly lists `operations: [CREATE, UPDATE]`.

**Validation logic**
- The old pattern-based `validate.pattern` (using Kyverno's `?*` wildcards and `=(optional):` syntax) is replaced with a CEL expression under `spec.validations[].expression`.
- A `variables` block collects all container types — `containers`, `initContainers`, and `ephemeralContainers` — using the optional chaining operator `?` and `orValue([])` so the expression is safe when those optional fields are absent.
- A `messageExpression` is added to produce a dynamic, per-container failure message (falling back to the static `message` if the expression errors).

  validationActions:
    - Audit
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ['']
        apiVersions: [v1]
        operations: [CREATE, UPDATE]
        resources: [pods]
  variables:
    - name: allContainers
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers
        + object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([])
        + object.spec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.allContainers.all(container,
          has(container.resources) &&
          has(container.resources.requests) &&
          has(container.resources.requests.memory) &&
          has(container.resources.requests.cpu) &&
          has(container.resources.limits) &&
          has(container.resources.limits.memory)
        )
      message: "CPU and memory resource requests and memory limits are required for containers."
      messageExpression: >-
        "Container '" +
        variables.allContainers.filter(container,
          !(has(container.resources) &&
            has(container.resources.requests) &&
            has(container.resources.requests.memory) &&
            has(container.resources.requests.cpu) &&
            has(container.resources.limits) &&
            has(container.resources.limits.memory))
        )[0].name +
        "' is missing required CPU/memory requests or memory limits."

description: a read-only root file system helps to enforce an immutable infrastructure strategy; the container only needs to write on the mounted volume that persists the state
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_require_ro_rootfs.yaml to a Kyverno 1.16+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy a read-only root file system helps to enforce an immutable infrastructure strategy; the container only needs to write on the mounted volume that persists the state. Write the converted policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/cp_require_ro_rootfs.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.16+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-ro-rootfs
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Read-Only Root Filesystem
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices, PSP Migration
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      A read-only root file system helps to enforce an immutable infrastructure strategy;
      the container only needs to write on the mounted volume that persists the state.
      An immutable root filesystem can also prevent malicious binaries from writing to the
      host system. This policy validates that containers define a securityContext
      with `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true`.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-readOnlyRootFilesystem
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Root filesystem must be read-only."
      pattern:
        spec:
          containers:
          - securityContext:
              readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-ro-rootfs
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Read-Only Root Filesystem
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices, PSP Migration
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.16.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      A read-only root file system helps to enforce an immutable infrastructure strategy;
      the container only needs to write on the mounted volume that persists the state.
      An immutable root filesystem can also prevent malicious binaries from writing to the
      host system. This policy validates that containers define a securityContext
      with `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true`.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ['']
        apiVersions: ['v1']
        operations: ['CREATE', 'UPDATE']
        resources: ['pods']
  variables:
    - name: allContainers
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers
        + object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([])
        + object.spec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.allContainers.all(c,
          has(c.securityContext) &&
          has(c.securityContext.readOnlyRootFilesystem) &&
          c.securityContext.readOnlyRootFilesystem == true
        )
      messageExpression: >-
        "Root filesystem must be read-only. Containers without readOnlyRootFilesystem=true: " +
        variables.allContainers.filter(c,
          !has(c.securityContext) ||
          !has(c.securityContext.readOnlyRootFilesystem) ||
          c.securityContext.readOnlyRootFilesystem == false
        ).map(c, c.name).join(", ")

**`apiVersion`** — Changed from `kyverno.io/v1` to `policies.kyverno.io/v1`, the new API group for Kyverno's policy types.

**`kind`** — Changed from `ClusterPolicy` to `ValidatingPolicy`. In the new API, `ValidatingPolicy` is cluster-scoped by default (there's also `NamespacedValidatingPolicy` for namespace-scoped use).

**`spec.validationFailureAction`** → **`spec.validationActions`** — The old scalar field is replaced by a list. `Audit` maps directly; `Enforce` would map to `Deny`.

The expression `object.spec.containers.all(container, container.?securityContext.?readOnlyRootFilesystem.orValue(false) == true)` uses the optional chaining operator (`?`) to safely traverse potentially absent fields and `orValue(false)` to default to `false` when `readOnlyRootFilesystem` is not set—preserving the exact same semantics as the original pattern rule.

**`policies.kyverno.io/minversion`** — Updated from `1.6.0` to `1.16.0` to reflect the minimum version required for `ValidatingPolicy`.

description: adding capabilities is a way for containers in a Pod to request higher levels of ability than those with which they may be provisioned
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_restrict_capabilities.yaml to a Kyverno 1.16+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy adding capabilities is a way for containers in a Pod to request higher levels of ability than those with which they may be provisioned. Write the converted policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/cp_restrict_capabilities.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.16+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: psp-restrict-adding-capabilities
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Restrict Adding Capabilities
    policies.kyverno.io/category: PSP Migration
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.23"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Adding capabilities is a way for containers in a Pod to request higher levels
      of ability than those with which they may be provisioned. Many capabilities
      allow system-level control and should be prevented. Pod Security Policies (PSP)
      allowed a list of "good" capabilities to be added. This policy checks
      ephemeralContainers, initContainers, and containers to ensure the only
      capabilities that can be added are either NET_BIND_SERVICE or CAP_CHOWN.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
    - name: allowed-capabilities
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
              - Pod
      preconditions:
        all:
        - key: "{{ request.operation || 'BACKGROUND' }}"
          operator: NotEquals
          value: DELETE
      validate:
        message: >-
          Any capabilities added other than NET_BIND_SERVICE or CAP_CHOWN are disallowed.
        foreach:
          - list: request.object.spec.[ephemeralContainers, initContainers, containers][]
            deny:
              conditions:
                all:
                - key: "{{ element.securityContext.capabilities.add[] || '' }}"
                  operator: AnyNotIn
                  value:
                  - NET_BIND_SERVICE
                  - CAP_CHOWN
                  - ''
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: psp-restrict-adding-capabilities
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Restrict Adding Capabilities
    policies.kyverno.io/category: PSP Migration
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.16.0
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.16.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.30+"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Adding capabilities is a way for containers in a Pod to request higher levels
      of ability than those with which they may be provisioned. Many capabilities
      allow system-level control and should be prevented. Pod Security Policies (PSP)
      allowed a list of "good" capabilities to be added. This policy checks
      ephemeralContainers, initContainers, and containers to ensure the only
      capabilities that can be added are either NET_BIND_SERVICE or CAP_CHOWN.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: true
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - ""
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - pods
  variables:
    - name: allContainers
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers
        + object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([])
        + object.spec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])
    - name: allowedCapabilities
      expression: "['NET_BIND_SERVICE', 'CAP_CHOWN']"
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.allContainers.all(container,
          container.?securityContext.?capabilities.?add.orValue([]).all(capability,
            capability in variables.allowedCapabilities))
      message: >-

apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: psp-restrict-adding-capabilities
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Restrict Adding Capabilities
    policies.kyverno.io/category: PSP Migration
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium

    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.16.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.30+"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Adding capabilities is a way for containers in a Pod to request higher levels
      of ability than those with which they may be provisioned. Many capabilities
      allow system-level control and should be prevented. Pod Security Policies (PSP)
      allowed a list of "good" capabilities to be added. This policy checks
      ephemeralContainers, initContainers, and containers to ensure the only
      capabilities that can be added are either NET_BIND_SERVICE or CAP_CHOWN.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: true
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:

        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - pods
  variables:
    - name: allContainers
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers
        + object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([])
        + object.spec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])
    - name: allowedCapabilities
      expression: "['NET_BIND_SERVICE', 'CAP_CHOWN']"
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.allContainers.all(container,
          container.?securityContext.?capabilities.?add.orValue([]).all(capability,
            capability in variables.allowedCapabilities))
      message: >-
        Any capabilities added other than NET_BIND_SERVICE or CAP_CHOWN are disallowed.

description: images from unknown, public registries can be of dubious quality and may not be scanned and secured, representing a high degree of risk
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_restrict_image_registries.yaml to a Kyverno 1.16+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy images from unknown, public registries can be of dubious quality and may not be scanned and secured, representing a high degree of risk. Write the converted policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/cp_restrict_image_registries.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.16+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: restrict-image-registries
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Restrict Image Registries
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.26"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Images from unknown, public registries can be of dubious quality and may not be
      scanned and secured, representing a high degree of risk. Requiring use of known, approved
      registries helps reduce threat exposure by ensuring image pulls only come from them. This
      policy validates that container images only originate from the registry `eu.foo.io` or
      `bar.io`. Use of this policy requires customization to define your allowable registries.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-registries
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Unknown image registry."
      pattern:
        spec:
          =(ephemeralContainers):
          - image: "eu.foo.io/* | bar.io/*"
          =(initContainers):
          - image: "eu.foo.io/* | bar.io/*"
          containers:
          - image: "eu.foo.io/* | bar.io/*"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: restrict-image-registries
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Restrict Image Registries
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.16.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.26"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Images from unknown, public registries can be of dubious quality and may not be
      scanned and secured, representing a high degree of risk. Requiring use of known, approved
      registries helps reduce threat exposure by ensuring image pulls only come from them. This
      policy validates that container images only originate from the registry `eu.foo.io` or
      `bar.io`. Use of this policy requires customization to define your allowable registries.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
  variables:
    - name: allContainers
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers
        + object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([])
        + object.spec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.allContainers.all(c,
          c.image.startsWith("eu.foo.io/") || c.image.startsWith("bar.io/")
        )
      messageExpression: >-
        "Unknown image registry. All images must originate from 'eu.foo.io' or 'bar.io'. Found invalid image(s): " +
        variables.allContainers.filter(c,
          !c.image.startsWith("eu.foo.io/") && !c.image.startsWith("bar.io/")
        ).map(c, c.image).join(", ")

- `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `kind: ValidatingPolicy`

**Spec restructuring:**
- `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` → `spec.validationActions: [Audit]` (the new list-based field)
- Removed `spec.background: true` — background scanning is enabled by default in `ValidatingPolicy`
- Replaced `spec.rules[].match` with `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` using the Kubernetes `ValidatingAdmissionPolicy`-style resource selector

- A dynamic `messageExpression` is added that names the specific non-conformant image(s) in the violation message, which is more actionable than the original static `"Unknown image registry."`

**Key conversions made:**

| Original (`kyverno.io/v1` ClusterPolicy) | Converted (`policies.kyverno.io/v1` ValidatingPolicy) |
|---|---|
| `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` | `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` |
| `kind: ClusterPolicy` | `kind: ValidatingPolicy` |
| `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` | `spec.validationActions: [Audit]` |
| `spec.background: true` | `spec.evaluation.background.enabled: true` |
| `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources` | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules[]` (VAP-style) |
| Pattern-based `validate.pattern` with `=(optional):` syntax | CEL `variables` + `validations[].expression` |

**Notable design decisions:**

- **`allContainers` variable** — collects all three container types (containers, initContainers, ephemeralContainers) using `?` optional field syntax and `orValue([])`, mirroring the original's `=()` optional container handling.
- **Kyverno Image library** — uses `image(c.image).registry()` from Kyverno's CEL image library to cleanly extract the registry hostname, then checks it against the two allowed registries (`eu.foo.io`, `bar.io`).
- **`messageExpression`** — provides a dynamic failure message listing the specific offending images, which is much more actionable than the original static `"Unknown image registry."`.
- **`policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.16.0`** — updated from `1.6.0` to reflect the minimum version required for `ValidatingPolicy`.

yaml_preview:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: unique-ingress-host
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Unique Ingress Host
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Ingress
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.16.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      An Ingress host is a URL at which services may be made available externally. In most cases,
      these hosts should be unique across the cluster to ensure no routing conflicts occur.
      This policy checks an incoming Ingress resource to ensure its hosts are unique to the cluster.
      It also ensures that only a single host may be specified in a given manifest.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: false
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ['networking.k8s.io']
        apiVersions: ['v1']
        operations: ['CREATE', 'UPDATE']
        resources: ['ingresses']
  variables:
    - name: incomingHosts
      expression: >-
        object.spec.rules.orValue([]).map(r, r.host)
    - name: allIngresses
      expression: >-
        resource.List("networking.k8s.io/v1", "ingresses", "").items
    - name: otherIngresses
      expression: >-
        variables.allIngresses.filter(i, i.metadata.uid != object.metadata.uid)
    - name: existingHosts
      expression: >-
        variables.otherIngresses.flatMap(i, i.spec.rules.orValue([]).map(r, r.host))
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.incomingHosts.size() <= 1
      message: "An Ingress resource may only contain a single host entry."
    - expression: >-
        !variables.incomingHosts.exists(h, h in variables.existingHosts)
      messageExpression: >-
        "The Ingress host name must be unique. Conflicting host(s): " +
        variables.incomingHosts.filter(h, h in variables.existingHosts).join(", ")
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: unique-ingress-host
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Unique Ingress Host
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Ingress
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      An Ingress host is a URL at which services may be made available externally. In most cases,
      these hosts should be unique across the cluster to ensure no routing conflicts occur.
      This policy checks an incoming Ingress resource to ensure its hosts are unique to the cluster.
      It also ensures that only a single host may be specified in a given manifest.      
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: false
  rules:
    - name: check-single-host-create
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
              - Ingress
      context:
        - name: hosts
          apiCall:
            urlPath: "/apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/ingresses"
            jmesPath: "items[].spec.rules[].host"
      preconditions:
        all:
        - key: "{{request.operation || 'BACKGROUND'}}"
          operator: Equals
          value: CREATE
      validate:
        message: "The Ingress host name must be unique."
        deny:
          conditions:
            all:
              - key: "{{ request.object.spec.rules[].host }}"
                operator: AnyIn
                value: "{{ hosts }}"
    - name: check-single-host-update
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
              - Ingress
      preconditions:
        all:
        - key: "{{request.operation || 'BACKGROUND'}}"
          operator: Equals
          value: UPDATE
      context:
        - name: allhosts
          apiCall:
            urlPath: "/apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/ingresses"
            jmesPath: "items[?metadata.uid!='{{ request.object.metadata.uid }}'].spec.rules[].host"
      validate:
        message: "The Ingress host name must be unique."
        deny:
          conditions:
            all:
              - key: "{{ request.object.spec.rules[].host }}"
                operator: AnyIn
                value: "{{ allhosts }}"
    - name: deny-multiple-hosts
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
              - Ingress
      preconditions:
        all:
        - key: "{{request.operation || 'BACKGROUND'}}"
          operator: AnyIn
          value:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        - key: "{{ request.object.spec.rules[].host | length(@)}}"
          operator: GreaterThan
          value: 1
      validate:
        message: "An Ingress resource may only contain a single host entry."
        deny: {}

raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made:

**API/Kind change:**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` + `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` + `kind: ValidatingPolicy`

**Enforcement:**
- `validationFailureAction: Audit` → `validationActions: [Audit]`
- `background: false` → `evaluation.background.enabled: false`

**Resource matching:**
- The three separate `match` blocks with `preconditions` are replaced by a single `matchConstraints.resourceRules` entry covering `CREATE` and `UPDATE` operations on `networking.k8s.io/v1` Ingresses

**Logic rewrite (JMESPath + deny → CEL + validations):**
- The old policy used separate rules for CREATE (compare against all hosts) and UPDATE (exclude current resource's UID), with a third rule to block multiple hosts
- The new policy uses `resource.List()` (Kyverno's CEL library) to fetch all Ingresses, then filters out the current object by UID via `variables.otherIngresses` — this handles both CREATE and UPDATE in one set of variables
- Two `validations` replace the three rules:
  1. Enforce at most one host per Ingress manifest
  2. Ensure no incoming host already exists in any other Ingress, with a dynamic `messageExpression` naming the conflicting host(s)

apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: unique-ingress-host
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Unique Ingress Host
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Ingress
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.14.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      An Ingress host is a URL at which services may be made available externally. In most cases,
      these hosts should be unique across the cluster to ensure no routing conflicts occur.
      This policy checks an incoming Ingress resource to ensure its hosts are unique to the cluster.
      It also ensures that only a single host may be specified in a given manifest.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: false
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ["networking.k8s.io"]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["ingresses"]
  variables:
    - name: allIngresses
      expression: >-
        resource.List("networking.k8s.io/v1", "ingresses", "").items
    - name: incomingHosts
      expression: >-
        object.spec.?rules.orValue([]).map(r, r.?host.orValue("")).filter(h, h != "")
    - name: existingHostsOnCreate
      expression: >-
        variables.allIngresses
          .map(i, i.spec.?rules.orValue([]).map(r, r.?host.orValue("")).filter(h, h != ""))
          .flatten()
    - name: existingHostsOnUpdate
      expression: >-
        variables.allIngresses
          .filter(i, i.metadata.uid != object.metadata.uid)
          .map(i, i.spec.?rules.orValue([]).map(r, r.?host.orValue("")).filter(h, h != ""))
          .flatten()
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        request.operation == "UPDATE"
          ? !variables.incomingHosts.exists(h, h in variables.existingHostsOnUpdate)
          : !variables.incomingHosts.exists(h, h in variables.existingHostsOnCreate)
      message: "The Ingress host name must be unique."
    - expression: >-
        variables.incomingHosts.size() <= 1
      message: "An Ingress resource may only contain a single host entry."

runs.raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of what changed and why:

**Key structural changes:**

| Old (ClusterPolicy) | New (ValidatingPolicy) |
|---|---|
| `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` | `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` |
| `kind: ClusterPolicy` | `kind: ValidatingPolicy` |
| `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` | `spec.validationActions: [Audit]` |
| `spec.background: false` | `spec.evaluation.background.enabled: false` |
| `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources` | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` |
| JMESPath `context` + `apiCall` | `variables` using CEL `resource.List()` |
| JMESPath `validate.deny.conditions` | CEL `validations[].expression` |
| Separate CREATE/UPDATE rules with `preconditions` | Single validation using `request.operation` ternary |

**Logic translation:**
- The three original rules (check-single-host-create, check-single-host-update, deny-multiple-hosts) are consolidated into **two CEL validations**:
  1. **Uniqueness check**: Uses `resource.List()` (Kyverno's CEL library for live API queries) to fetch all cluster Ingresses, then branches on `request.operation` to either exclude the current resource's UID (UPDATE) or check all hosts (CREATE).
  2. **Single-host check**: Ensures at most one host is declared in the incoming Ingress manifest.

The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes and design decisions:

**Structural changes (`kyverno.io/v1` → `policies.kyverno.io/v1`):**
- `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `kind: ValidatingPolicy` (cluster-scoped by default)
- `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` → `spec.validationActions: [Audit]`
- `spec.rules[]` with JMESPath/deny conditions → `spec.validations[]` with CEL expressions
- `spec.background: false` → `spec.evaluation.background.enabled: false`
- `match.any[].resources.kinds` → `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules[]`

**Logic consolidation:**
The three original rules (CREATE check, UPDATE check, deny-multiple-hosts) are consolidated into two `validations` entries using CEL:

1. **Single-host check** — `variables.incomingHosts.size() <= 1` replaces the `deny-multiple-hosts` rule with its precondition on host count.

2. **Unique-host check** — Uses `resource.List()` (Kyverno's CEL library for live API calls) to fetch all existing Ingresses, filters out the current object by UID (covering both CREATE where UID is absent and UPDATE where it must be excluded), then checks that no incoming host appears in the existing hosts. This merges the separate CREATE/UPDATE rules into one expression.

runs.yaml_preview:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: unique-ingress-host
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Unique Ingress Host
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Ingress
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.16.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      An Ingress host is a URL at which services may be made available externally. In most cases,
      these hosts should be unique across the cluster to ensure no routing conflicts occur.
      This policy checks an incoming Ingress resource to ensure its hosts are unique to the cluster.
      It also ensures that only a single host may be specified in a given manifest.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: false
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ['networking.k8s.io']
        apiVersions: ['v1']
        operations: ['CREATE', 'UPDATE']
        resources: ['ingresses']
  variables:
    - name: incomingHosts
      expression: >-
        object.spec.rules.orValue([]).map(r, r.host)
    - name: allIngresses
      expression: >-
        resource.List("networking.k8s.io/v1", "ingresses", "").items
    - name: otherIngresses
      expression: >-
        variables.allIngresses.filter(i, i.metadata.uid != object.metadata.uid)
    - name: existingHosts
      expression: >-
        variables.otherIngresses.flatMap(i, i.spec.rules.orValue([]).map(r, r.host))
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.incomingHosts.size() <= 1
      message: "An Ingress resource may only contain a single host entry."
    - expression: >-
        !variables.incomingHosts.exists(h, h in variables.existingHosts)
      messageExpression: >-
        "The Ingress host name must be unique. Conflicting host(s): " +
        variables.incomingHosts.filter(h, h in variables.existingHosts).join(", ")

The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made:

**API/Kind change:**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` + `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` + `kind: ValidatingPolicy`

**Enforcement:**
- `validationFailureAction: Audit` → `validationActions: [Audit]`
- `background: false` → `evaluation.background.enabled: false`

**Resource matching:**
- The three separate `match` blocks with `preconditions` are replaced by a single `matchConstraints.resourceRules` entry covering `CREATE` and `UPDATE` operations on `networking.k8s.io/v1` Ingresses

**Logic rewrite (JMESPath + deny → CEL + validations):**
- The old policy used separate rules for CREATE (compare against all hosts) and UPDATE (exclude current resource's UID), with a third rule to block multiple hosts
- The new policy uses `resource.List()` (Kyverno's CEL library) to fetch all Ingresses, then filters out the current object by UID via `variables.otherIngresses` — this handles both CREATE and UPDATE in one set of variables
- Two `validations` replace the three rules:
  1. Enforce at most one host per Ingress manifest
  2. Ensure no incoming host already exists in any other Ingress, with a dynamic `messageExpression` naming the conflicting host(s)

description: a Kubernetes Service of type NodePort uses a host port to receive traffic from any source
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_restrict_nodeport.yaml to a Kyverno 1.16+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy a Kubernetes Service of type NodePort uses a host port to receive traffic from any source. Write the converted policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/cp_restrict_nodeport.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.16+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: restrict-nodeport
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow NodePort
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Service
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      A Kubernetes Service of type NodePort uses a host port to receive traffic from
      any source. A NetworkPolicy cannot be used to control traffic to host ports.
      Although NodePort Services can be useful, their use must be limited to Services
      with additional upstream security checks. This policy validates that any new Services
      do not use the `NodePort` type.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-nodeport
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Service
    validate:
      message: "Services of type NodePort are not allowed."
      pattern:
        spec:
          =(type): "!NodePort"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: restrict-nodeport
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow NodePort
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.16.0
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Service
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      A Kubernetes Service of type NodePort uses a host port to receive traffic from
      any source. A NetworkPolicy cannot be used to control traffic to host ports.
      Although NodePort Services can be useful, their use must be limited to Services
      with additional upstream security checks. This policy validates that any new Services
      do not use the `NodePort` type.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["services"]
  validations:
    - message: "Services of type NodePort are not allowed."
      expression: "!has(object.spec.type) || object.spec.type != 'NodePort'"
raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key changes made during conversion:

| Aspect | Original (`kyverno.io/v1` ClusterPolicy) | Converted (`policies.kyverno.io/v1` ValidatingPolicy) |
|---|---|---|
| `apiVersion` | `kyverno.io/v1` | `policies.kyverno.io/v1` |
| `kind` | `ClusterPolicy` | `ValidatingPolicy` |
| `validationFailureAction: Audit` | top-level in `spec` | `spec.validationActions: [Audit]` |
| `background: true` | explicit field | handled via `spec.evaluation.background` (default enabled) |
| Resource matching | `spec.rules[].match` with `kinds` | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` (CEL/VAP style) |
| Validation rule | `validate.pattern` with Kyverno pattern syntax (`=(type): "!NodePort"`) | `spec.validations[].expression` using CEL: `!has(object.spec.type) \|\| object.spec.type != 'NodePort'` |
| `minversion` annotation | `1.6.0` | updated to `1.16.0` |

The CEL expression `!has(object.spec.type) || object.spec.type != 'NodePort'` correctly handles both cases: Services where `spec.type` is absent (defaults to `ClusterIP`, which is allowed) and Services where it is explicitly set to something other than `NodePort`.

Key design decisions:
- **CEL expression**: The original Kyverno pattern `=(type): "!NodePort"` (conditional field check) maps directly to the CEL ternary `has(object.spec.type) ? (object.spec.type != 'NodePort') : true`, which passes when `type` is absent (defaults to `ClusterIP`) and denies when it is `NodePort`.
- **Operations**: Added explicit `CREATE` and `UPDATE` operations to the `matchConstraints`, which is required by the VAP-style schema and matches the intent of the original policy.

description: mutates Pods and Services to add the label 'foo: bar' to metadata.labels. The label must be added if absent and left unchanged if already present.
prompt:
Write a Kyverno 1.16+ MutatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1) that mutates Pods and Services to add the label 'foo: bar' to metadata.labels. The label must be added if absent and left unchanged if already present. Write the policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/cursor/gen_add_default_labels.yaml.

Look up Kyverno 1.16+ documentation and examples before writing the policy:
- https://kyverno.io/docs/
- https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: MutatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-foo-bar-label
spec:
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ['']
        apiVersions: ['v1']
        operations: ['CREATE', 'UPDATE']
        resources: ['pods', 'services']
  mutations:
    - patchType: ApplyConfiguration
      applyConfiguration:
        expression: >
          Object{
            metadata: Object.metadata{
              labels: Object.metadata.labels{
                foo: "bar"
              }
            }
          }
raw_log:
The policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's what it does and why each choice was made:

**`patchType: ApplyConfiguration`** — uses a merge-style patch (not JSONPatch). The `Object{}` CEL expression merges into the existing resource, so:
- If `foo` is **absent**, it gets added with value `"bar"`.
- If `foo` is **already present** with any value, the merge leaves it unchanged (ApplyConfiguration only sets fields not already present when using this pattern with Kubernetes server-side apply semantics).

**`resources: ['pods', 'services']`** — targets both Pods and Services in a single rule under the core API group (`apiGroups: ['']`, `apiVersions: ['v1']`).

**`operations: ['CREATE', 'UPDATE']`** — applies the label on both resource creation and updates.
